title: Unraveling the Mystery of Galaxy Motions with a Cosmic Sheet
summary: Astronomers have identified a massive, flattened structure of matter surrounding the Local Group, which includes the Milky Way and Andromeda galaxies. This cosmic sheet, spanning tens of millions of light-years, comprises both ordinary matter and dark matter. It explains a persistent mystery: why most large galaxies near the Local Group are receding rather than being gravitationally attracted. For about fifty years, this anomaly challenged scientists, as the combined mass of the Local Group should pull nearby galaxies inward. An international team led by Ewoud Wempe used advanced computer simulations to investigate. They started with early universe conditions from cosmic microwave background data and evolved a model to present day, creating a virtual twin of our cosmic environment. The simulations accurately replicate the positions and velocities of 31 galaxies outside the Local Group. The sheet's distributed mass counterbalances the Local Group's gravity, causing galaxies within the plane to move away. Regions above and below the sheet are cosmic voids with few galaxies, accounting for the lack of inward motion. This finding aligns with cosmological models and provides insights into dark matter distribution based on galaxy dynamics.

a theoretical framework describing the universe's origin, evolution, and large-scale structure

background_read: In cosmology, the universe's expansion was discovered by Edwin Hubble, showing galaxies receding due to the Big Bang. Exceptions like Andromeda moving toward the Milky Way highlight local gravitational effects. The Local Group is a small galaxy cluster whose gravity should attract nearby objects, but observations show many galaxies moving away, posing a decades-long puzzle. Dark matter, inferred from gravitational effects, makes up about 27% of the universe's mass. Computer simulations use initial conditions from the cosmic microwave background to model structure formation. Cosmic voids are large underdense regions influencing large-scale dynamics. This study integrates these elements to explain local galaxy motions through mass distribution.
Article_Structure: Main Points: The study identifies a cosmic sheet of matter around the Local Group that explains why nearby galaxies recede instead of being gravitationally pulled. Purpose: To solve a long-standing astronomical mystery using simulations. Evidence Evaluation: High-quality evidence from computer models that replicate observed galaxy positions and velocities, based on cosmic microwave background data. Author Credibility: Led by Ewoud Wempe from the Kapteyn Institute, with international collaboration and support from the University of Groningen. Methodology: Advanced simulations starting from early universe conditions, evolved to match present-day observations, creating a virtual twin environment.

perspectives.description: The research advances understanding of local mass distribution and aligns with broader cosmological models.
